summary: I first became involved in the Civil Rights Movement during our North Carolina Black Belt campaign as a Field Organizer for the Southern Conference Educational Fund. We faced intense opposition from groups like the Klan and the John Birch Society, especially in places like Halifax and Bertie counties. Our work included grassroots meetings, boycotts, and voter registration drives that led to a major federal court victory in Raleigh. The movement was challenging but rewarding as we helped register thousands of Black and Indian voters for the first time since Reconstruction.

Bertie County was a key area where we organized a large Civil Rights and Anti-Poverty Conference in early March 1965 at the Indian Woods Baptist Church. Although the Klan was present, their activity was limited to cross-burnings, and we maintained the initiative. After the conference, local leaders like Rev. W.M. Steele invited me to stay and organize, which led to successful actions like the march demanding Federal food commodities and the Food Stamp Program. Bertie was a challenging but pivotal county in our broader Black Belt organizing efforts.

I served as a Field Organizer for the Southern Conference Educational Fund during our North Carolina Black Belt campaign. This organization was central to our efforts in Halifax, Bertie, and other counties, where we conducted grassroots meetings, boycotts, and voter registration drives. The Fund helped us coordinate multi-county activism and brought in key speakers like Ella Baker for our conferences. It was through this role that I was able to push forward civil rights and anti-poverty initiatives in the region.

Halifax County was where we started our North Carolina Black Belt campaign west of Bertie, facing fierce resistance from groups like the Klan and the John Birch Society. It was like trench warfare, with violence and economic reprisals against activists. Despite this, we held meetings and demonstrations that culminated in a sweeping voter registration victory in Federal Court in Raleigh. The success in Halifax spread hope and momentum to other counties in the Black Belt.

The Ku Klux Klan was a constant threat during our organizing efforts in Halifax and Bertie counties. In Halifax, they were part of a tightly organized opposition to social change, alongside groups like the Birch Society and North Carolina Defenders of States Rights. In Bertie, their presence was less strong but they still tried to intimidate us with cross-burnings. Despite their intimidation tactics, we maintained the initiative and continued our work.

The John Birch Society was one of the reactionary groups opposing our civil rights work in Halifax County during the 1960s. Alongside the Klan and North Carolina Defenders of States Rights, they formed a tightly organized front against social change. Their presence made organizing difficult and dangerous, but we persisted with grassroots meetings and voter registration drives. Their opposition underscored the challenges we faced in the Black Belt region.
cues: North Carolina Defenders of States Rights

entity: North Carolina Defenders of States Rights
summary: The North Carolina Defenders of States Rights were part of the organized resistance to civil rights activism in Halifax County. Along with the Klan and the John Birch Society, they opposed any social change that threatened the status quo. Their presence made our work feel like trench warfare, but we continued with grassroots meetings, boycotts, and voter registration efforts. Overcoming their opposition was crucial to our eventual victories in the region.

Our voter registration drives in Halifax and Bertie counties were historic because they registered thousands of Black and Indian voters for the first time since the Reconstruction era. This period after the Civil War had originally enfranchised Black citizens, but many had been disenfranchised since then. Our federal court victory in Raleigh helped restore these voting rights decades later. It was a powerful moment connecting past struggles to our present fight for equality.

Raleigh was the site of a major federal court victory that validated our voter registration efforts in Halifax and Bertie counties. After facing intense opposition and violence, we won a sweeping voter rights case there that allowed thousands of Black and Indian citizens to register to vote for the first time since Reconstruction. Raleigh was also where we negotiated with county officials to approve federal food programs. It was a critical hub for legal and political progress in our movement.

The Black Belt region of North Carolina was the focus of our multi-county organizing campaign during the 1960s. Starting in Halifax County and moving into Bertie and Northampton, we worked to build grassroots activism among Black and Indian communities. The region was marked by entrenched opposition from groups like the Klan and conservative political forces, but it was also a place where we achieved significant voter registration and anti-poverty victories. The Black Belt was both a battleground and a beacon for civil rights progress.

We organized a major Civil Rights and Anti-Poverty Conference at the Indian Woods Baptist Church in Bertie County in early March 1965. Though initially only the clergyman's car was there, over a thousand people from 14 counties arrived to participate. The church served as a rural gathering place for workshops and speeches, including a keynote by Ella Baker and music by Clyde Appleton. The event was hailed as a major regional success and helped catalyze further organizing in Bertie.

My old friend Clyde Appleton was a key part of the Civil Rights and Anti-Poverty Conference at Indian Woods Baptist Church, where he contributed the freedom music dimension. His involvement helped energize the crowd of over a thousand attendees from 14 counties. Appleton's music was an important cultural element that complemented the speeches and workshops, including those led by Ella Baker. He brought a spirit of freedom and solidarity to the event.

Ella Baker was the keynote speaker at our Civil Rights and Anti-Poverty Conference held in March 1965 at Indian Woods Baptist Church in Bertie County. Her presence brought great inspiration to the over 1,000 people gathered from 14 counties. Baker was a longtime friend and a major figure in the movement, and her leadership helped galvanize grassroots activism in the Black Belt region. Having her speak was a highlight of the conference and helped legitimize our efforts.

Rev. W. M. Steele was a key local leader in Bertie County who approached me after the conference and urged us to come organize there quickly. Steele had been a rural school teacher but was fired for teaching students about math and how sharecroppers were exploited by plantation owners. He became an important ally in our efforts to build grassroots power in Bertie. Together, we met with other local leaders and the Ministerial Alliance to begin organizing.

The Tuscarora people were among the communities we organized in Bertie County, alongside Black sharecroppers. We included them in marches and demands for federal food commodities and the Food Stamp Program. Their participation was vital in building a broad coalition for social and economic justice in the county. Engaging the Tuscarora helped strengthen our multi-ethnic grassroots base in the Black Belt region.

After our successes in Bertie County, we expanded our organizing efforts into Northampton County and other nearby areas. The work in Northampton built on the momentum from Bertie and Halifax, continuing our multi-county strategy in the Black Belt. Each county presented unique challenges, but overall the organizing went very well. Northampton was part of our broader vision to transform the region through grassroots activism.

One of the major issues we tackled in Bertie County was the refusal of the County Board of Commissioners to approve the new Federal Food Stamp Program. Planters opposed it because they wanted to keep sharecroppers dependent, while urban merchants had commercial interests. We organized nearly six hundred sharecroppers and Tuscaroras to march on the courthouse demanding approval. After negotiations, the Board approved the Food Stamp Program, marking a significant victory for economic justice.
